title: Barbecue Sauce on Cotton
stainChemistry: BBQ sauce is one of the stickiest combination stains because it has every category at once: tomato (red color plus tannin), sugar (a lot of it — and sugar caramelizes under heat, which becomes extraordinarily sticky and almost permanent), vinegar (mild acid, cotton-friendly), spice pigments (yellow-orange from turmeric and paprika), and oil (cooking oil plus sometimes spattered grill oil). The sugar is the trap. BBQ is a summer stain — sun on a hanging shirt can caramelize the sugar without any iron touching it. Cotton tolerates the full toolkit (Dawn, mild acid, 3% peroxide, sunlight, hot wash) AFTER chemistry has done its work. The order is sugar flush first (long cold rinse), then oil strip, then color removal.
whyThisWorks: A long cold-water flush dissolves the sugar before it caramelizes — that's what locks the rest of the stain in. Dawn strips the oil so chemistry can reach the pigment underneath. Mild acid (vinegar) disrupts the tomato color. 3% peroxide plus sunlight handles the residual yellow from spice pigments. Each component has its own step; in sequence, the whole stain lifts. Skip the sugar flush or apply heat early and everything locks in.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): BBQ sauce combines tomato (lycopene + tannin), high sugar load (caramelizes above ~150°C), turmeric (curcumin), paprika (capsanthin) carotenoids, and oil. Pro uses long cold flush + NSD + acid-side tannin spotter + 3% H₂O₂ + UV-assist for curcumin photodegradation.
defaultAssumption: BBQ sauce stains are almost always summer-context: picnics, cookouts, backyard. Assume the stain has had some sun exposure, which means the sugar may have started caramelizing. Treat as moderately aged unless the user says they're catching it within minutes.

spottingProtocol.technique: Remove bulk, cold flush from back
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dull blade, cold water, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape solid sauce off with a dull blade — don't rub. Cold flush from the BACK of the fabric, LONGER than a standard flush (90 seconds+) because the sugar needs to dissolve before it caramelizes further. Cold only — heat accelerates the sugar-lock AND the lycopene deepening.
Apply NSD, work with a soft bristle brush. NSD handles the sauce's oil fraction, the remaining sugar residue, and starts lifting surface pigment.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A) — acid-side tannin treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Acid-side tannin spotter by dropper on the residual red/brown. Disrupts the tomato-tannin bond. Dwell 3–5 min, blot.

Apply 3% H₂O₂ to the residual orange-red (lycopene) and yellow (turmeric/paprika) shadow. For aged stains, hang damp in sunlight — UV accelerates lycopene + curcumin oxidation. Fresh stains often don't need the sun step.
Rinse until water runs clear. Launder hot per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.

steps: 1. Scrape bulk. Cold flush LONGER than usual — dissolve sugar before caramelization.
2. NSD — handles oil + residual sugar + surface pigment.
3. Acid-side tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter / PRENETT-A). 3–5 min.
4. 3% H₂O₂ + sun assist on residual lycopene / turmeric / paprika pigment.
5. NSD flush, hot wash per care label.
products: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold flush (longer than standard); cool through chemistry; hot wash post-chemistry; sun for aged pigment
warnings: Sugar flush is longer than a regular tannin protocol — caramelization is the lock
Heat (sun, iron, dryer) accelerates the lock; no heat until chemistry has cleared the sugar
Multiple pigments (tomato + turmeric + paprika) may need multiple oxidizer passes
One oxidizer at a time, NSD-flushed between

homeSolutions.instruction: Scrape any solid sauce off with a dull butter knife. Then hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water for at least 90 seconds — longer than you'd flush most stains. BBQ sauce has a lot of sugar and you're racing the caramelization reaction. Cold only.

BBQ sauce has oil in it (plus sometimes grill oil spatter). Work a drop of Dawn dish soap into the stain with a fingertip or soft toothbrush, let sit 10 minutes, rinse cool. Should see the stain lighten noticeably as the oil comes off.

homeSolutions.agent: White Vinegar Dab for the Red
homeSolutions.instruction: For residual red/brown: mix 1 teaspoon white vinegar with 4 tablespoons cool water. Dab on the spot, let sit 3 minutes, blot. Works on the tomato tannin.

For stubborn orange-yellow shadow (from turmeric / paprika): dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on the spot, let it bubble 5 min, blot. For aged stains, hang the damp fabric in direct sunlight for 30–60 min — UV helps fade the carotenoid pigments. Skip peroxide on dark or dyed cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia — toxic fume risk.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. If you still see the stain, repeat the Dawn + vinegar + peroxide round.

If the BBQ sauce has been hot-dried or two home rounds haven't cleared it, take to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "BBQ sauce on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + vinegar + 3% H₂O₂ + sun."

steps: 2. LONG cold flush from the BACK — 90+ seconds to dissolve sugar before it caramelizes.
3. Dawn first — strips the oil. 10 min dwell, rinse cool.
4. Diluted white vinegar for residual red.
5. 3% hydrogen peroxide + sunlight for orange-yellow shadow. Skip on dark cotton.
6. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
7. Escalate if heat-set or two rounds failed.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if BBQ sauce has been hot-dried or two home rounds haven't cleared it.
warnings: Longer cold flush than regular tannin — caramelization is the risk
Heat = sugar-lock. No hot water, no sun drying, no iron until chemistry clears
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia

neverDo: Never hot-flush BBQ sauce — sugar caramelizes + lycopene sets
Never skip the long cold flush — sugar is the lock
Never layer oxidizers — one at a time
Never dry with residual pigment — heat sets it
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates NSD, acid-side spotters, 3% H₂O₂, sunlight, hot wash
Dyed cotton: skip or inside-seam-test peroxide

materialWarnings: Long cold flush — sugar lock is the enemy
Heat in any form sets BBQ sauce harder
Peroxide on whites; test inside seam on dark cotton

escalation.when: BBQ sauce heat-set by iron, hot dryer, or extended sun on a hanging garment; or two home rounds don't clear it.
escalation.whatToTell: "BBQ sauce on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older, possibly sun-exposed], home-treated with Dawn + vinegar + 3% H₂O₂ + sun."

contextQuestions: How long ago did the spill happen?
Has the fabric been in direct sun or gone through a hot wash since?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
Sweet-style BBQ (more sugar → harder), vinegar-style (easier), or mustard-based (more turmeric, plateau risk)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: BBQ sauce is the summer stain where the biggest enemy is sun / heat finishing the caramelization while the user drives home from the cookout. Cold flush ASAP, even just with a water bottle at the event, is a real win.
